commentBody:
Four one I would like to say,

Great xword, absolutely no sports knowledge needed whatsoever, purely delightful.
As easy as it gets. Just missed by PB because of a typo!
Please fix the glitch that makes the puzzle quit several times while I’m in the process of solving it.

Something about this one didn't ring true for me.  The animals involved are, as I think of them, furry, not FUZZY.  My beard after a few days without shaving is FUZZY.   A navel full of lint is FUZZY.  Some people's logic is FUZZY.  But I don't find anything particularly FUZZY about dogs, bears, etc.

And before you tell me that FUZZY Wuzzy was a bear, remember that FUZZY Wuzzy wasn't very FUZZY.

Of course, all fine for crosswords...I just wasn't taken by this theme.

Another Monday, albeit a Lively one. 
It should make many solvers feel all WARM AND FUZZY.
I'll skip the HOT DOGS and take the BABA ghanouj.
What will the ORACLE at Delphi say about this one?

For one reason or another, the Part One Durham Wordplay Brunch this summer was only three of us--Maggie, Jim, and me--but we enjoyed catching up with each other. Brunch was at a Chapel Hill restaurant that flooded badly last year around July 4 and only recently reopened, so we were happy to give them some business. You'll notice I said Part One up there; that's because it looks like there will be a Part Two get-together in early August when David Connell will be in the area. No definite plans on that one yet, and someone else will have to report on it, as I will likely be out of town. Stay cool, everybody!
Ice bag? Really? And, oh yeah, ban the Oreo!

Easy but fun puzzle from Brad, with some fresh clues and entries and a nice theme set.

Had SNOW before OREO, then of course it came around again. Nice OREO clue!

GETUP over AWAKE—sweet! Too bad they didn’t relate those clues, but I guess they often avoid that on a Tuesday.

A lot of pointed things in the North Pole. SHOES didn’t fit, and EARS aren’t exactly an accessory…

I didn’t even see the clue for CATIO (solved the downs) but good job not just going for the easy ARE/RATIO cross, way to keep it Brad Lively…I mean, keep it lively, Brad…

It strikes me that IMDOWN could be clued as “last vertical entry for a very large Ancient Roman crossword?”

Last suggestion for a theme entry: “Annual Nevada tradition” BURNING MAN (some of us are fuzzy chested!)

I thought I bought peaches yesterday, but my memory's fuzzy.

(This joke is the pits.)
My Diary of a Crossword Fiend review: https://crosswordfiend.com/2026/07/06/tuesday-july-7-2026/#ny

I loved this one! Day 555 of my streak.

Unfortunately, I missed the theme until after I completed the puzzle, but it indeed made me feel WARM AND FUZZY.

What tickled me pink, however, was the clever cluing. For instance it's always pointed at the north pole, what (not) to call people, cooler in a cooler and hole foods, to name a few. These definitely made me chuckle. Thanks to the constructor!
Need to mention: MUSH is not a sled dog command anywhere except in the comics. The three main commands are GEE, HAW and HIKE (for left, right and start). Otherwise a fun solve.

Your Santa is known in most regions of Poland as Święty Mikołaj, Saint Nicholas, a name we don't shorten, as we generally don't share the American penchant for abbreviation. He works alone rather than with elfs, and out of Finnish Lapland rather than the North Pole, so I needed loads of crosses to get the ELF HATS clue. In the Western Wielkopolska region the equivalent of Święty Mikołaj is Gwiazdor (roughly, Star-Man), a non-religious figure, originating from traditional processions of carolers, which were led by a guy dressed in sheep's fur worn inside-out, and carrying a star on a long pole.

The ref may be the yellow (or red) card wielder, but apparently politics may trump the rules of the game, requiring karma to intervene.

Why is a LIVE ONE an easy mark for a hustle? Dead people are unhustleable I suppose but that can't be it 🤣 (seriously though, I don't get it - please help me understand)

Was like a capybara in a hot spring for most of this puzzle

Only tough crossing was 60A/36D
After solving 50A, I was certain 20A was going to be BEACH PUPPIES. LOL
I had never heard of the answers to 7 down or 15 across so had to resort to Googling to finish. Otherwise a quick solve with some cute clues.

Tee hee... I thought 13D was a little tough for a Tuesday. A 20th century Chinese political figure, maybe? (It took me years to remember Zhou Enlai.) Nope, easier than that.

In conclusion, life is zany, complex, and disconcerting, often sending us into dreamlike states that do not facilitate xword solving. Let's embrace every moment if we can.

I don't know what happened to the dog, but:

The bunny, the fox and the bear appeared at their pre-induction physical after being drafted.

They weren't too pleased and brainstormed about how to get out of it.  

In a fit of last minute desperation the bunny turns to the fox: "Fox, I have a plan!  Bite off my ear!"

The fox reluctantly does so and the bunny enters the office. A few moments later, he gets out yelling "I was rejected guys!!"

"Because of your ear?" they ask. "Yes, because without it I can't detect the enemy as well" says Bunny. 

With that in mind, right before the fox is called, he turns to the bear, grits his teeth, and blurts "Rip my tail off!!". 

The bear does what needs to be done. Then the fox takes his turn in the office. After a while he comes back yelling "I was rejected too!  Without my tail, they said, I can't be as sneaky and agile as I need to be".

Next up was the bear.  "Quickly guys knock out all of my teeth.  A bear without teeth isn't dangerous at all!" 

The rabbit and the fox start beating the muzzle of the bear, completly breaking his face untill there is no tooth is left in his mouth. He then proceeds to go inside the doctors office. When he gets out he shouts "Rejecwew!!.

"Nice" they say. "Because of your teeth, right?" 

"Nwo" says the bear "Too fat"

Nice, fun Tuesday outing. TIL CATIO. Not being a kitty lover this arrangement has passed me by.
We’re currently hosting our beloved grandson and the family dog, LEIA. Parents are obsessed with SW. we half expected their child to be called Andor or Han. Quite relieved he isn’t.

The UK is sweltering in another heatwave this week. Not quite as bad as the last one, don’t think we’ll hit 100F but it’s close. Very high humidity, long daylight hours and no a/c anywhere still make it a test of endurance. We’re haymaking this afternoon, so the next few days are going to be brutal.
A Lively tune for this Lively Tuesday puzzle. https://youtu.be/bDnNqsy7rYE?si=EBjh0eWbbfh69znP
Well, any day I wake up alive is a reason to feel WARMANDFUZZY.  As to fuzzy, that reminds me, I need to shave....

I have a question about the search function and wonder if it's just my device or browser.

If I search on a users screen name I get replies to that name if there are any, and all comments that include the consecutive string of letters in the user name, but not the original comment from the user. Sometimes I like to look for particular posters, and unless I remember what they use as their secondary identifier (state name or whatever), I can't locate them.

Is this a me problem?

For me Monday and Tuesday were flipped.
Fun puzzle. I didn't catch the hook of the theme 'till I read Sam's column. 
32A. Great clue for you know what!
Regular clues I usually misspell: "Star Wars" princess. "Wicked" witch.
Omgggg what a silly little theme! <333

Brad's puzzles have bounce, that is, they don’t feel like “just another puzzle”.  The themes feel anything but stale, there are clues that have angles not seen before, not to mention a handful of lovely debut and non-debut answers.

Brad’s puzzles delightfully ring with his voice.  He sure has the attention of the editors – this is his third puzzle in less than six weeks!

His themes showcase language-quirk patterns not seen in puzzles before, and they are tight.  When a theme isn’t tight, you always see worthy alternate theme answers in the comments, but today, spare to none.

As for his unique cluing, may I present [What to call people … or not to call people], which makes bland answer NAMES stand out.

As for lovely debut answers, there are a pair that I can’t believe have never appeared in the Times before, BEACH BUNNIES and WARM AND FUZZY.  As for lovely non-debut answers – I’M DOWN and LIVE ONE.

Your puzzles are anything but ho-hum, Brad.  Bravo, sir, thank you, and more please!

Just missing Lennon’s second wife, and chocolate sandwich cookie to complete the crosswordese bingo card. 

I liked it.
Wait, this is such a cute theme!!! It never occurred to me there could be so many different warm and fuzzy things :D

Meh. This one left me feeling all warm and furry.

Though I have a sudden hankering for peach flambé.
Really fun one. The theme was cute and helped solve the puzzle. Enjoyed this a lot!

Fun puzzle. Had to work the crosses and ponder for some of the theme answers, but that just made for an enjoyable workout.  And the 'reveal' was one of the last things I worked out, so that was a nice 'aha' moment. 

And puzzle find today was inspired by current events and a search for an old parody title of a Beach Boys hit. Well - nope, it was never an answer, but that did lead me to one puzzle find. A Thursday from October 14, 1998 by David J. Kahn. One answer was: 

THEBEACHBOYS
And the other theme answers referenced that answer. Some of those: 

CALIFORNIAGIRLS
HELPMERHONDA
CAROLINENO 

And the one that led me there: 

BARBARAANN 

Some of you will get that. 

....

Fun quick solve. 

Rating:
8/10

I think we have a basic disagreement over whether an animal's coat should be accurately termed "smooth and silken" rather than FUZZY.  I base this on close aquaintance with a gray FOX and a (rescued) nest of baby BUNNIES.  

New definition/usage of MOJO:  Charisma.  
Very glad for the Crosses with some entries (7D, especially.  Who??)

Two long seams away from completing the center of a quilt top.  Then the borders...

I found the theme adorable and the theme answers very well chosen.  Once again -- I think you have something that a computer program can't do and that requires human imagination and ingenuity.  Always a Very Good Thing to me.

A couple of thoughts.  I don't remember ever hearing the term BEACH BUNNIES, but evidently they are gals who go to the beach and DO surf.  Whereas SNOW BUNNIES are people who go to ski resorts but DON'T ski.  Isn't that odd?

I don't know exactly what a LIVE ONE is or why they are an "easy mark for a hustle."  I think you'd be an easier mark for a hustle if you were half dead or ON AUTO.

Could this be a first for this OREO clue?  I do think it's Weird indeed to write an entire song about a cookie.  Is that what Al did?

Cute theme with a bit more crunch than the average Tuesday.  I enjoyed it.

As is often the case, "Amazing Grace" properly refers only to the lyrics--the tune commonly associated with it is named "New Britain."* Both have interesting back-stories. "Amazing Grace," first published in England in 1779, was penned by John Newton, a merchant seaman involved in the Atlantic slave trade, later turned Anglican pastor and staunch abolitionist. "Amazing Grace" recounts his personal conversion from one to the other. "New Britain," (named after the town in Connecticut, most likely) was composed/adapted/arranged by the American singing teacher William "Singing Billy" Walker, and published by him in his tunebook *Southern Harmony*, in 1835. As such, it is part of the "Shape-note" tradition, with its distinct harmonic language. I couldn't find any good YouTubes of Walker's original harmonization--which would have been in three parts--but here's one form the more popular *Sacred Harp*, in which a fourth, alto, part has been added: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pgSt5vnN3h4

I may have missed it, but did they reduce the allowed time to earn a gold star? I just finished the Sunday puzzle with no hints and got a blue star. It’s happened to me several times lately. I always thought you had 48 hours from publishing time.

I must be getting a little better at these! I’m nearly 700 solves in so Monday and Tuesday are usually a breeze now. What made this puzzle so great was the cluing! A new Oreo clue and what feels like the millionth time for Elan - but clued just a bit better than I recently remember. Missed my best time by a few (mostly because my warm and fuzzy Boston Terrier kept trying to get through my fence and the puzzle stays open when I jump up to get him.) 

Stay cool - maybe avoid getting hot dogs at Coney Island this week! (There was definitely something fuzzy in my fries last time I was there!)

Enjoyed this light and fun puzzle. 

My background music today is Kelly, our piano tuner, trying to bring my piano down a notch. It is a bit sharp today, but, as it is 50 years old, hasn't it earned a bit of sharpness? That's what the ladies in the senior living where I last worked used to think. "We're old, we have earned the right to be mean," they told the activities director. "Even if it hurts my feelings?" she asked. That gave them pause; everyone loved her. "Well, not YOU," they qualified. I think they were nice to her for the next hour or two, until their filters melted away once again.
That's too bad, I was looking forward to trying an AfACADO.
This was lots of fun! Thanks Brad :-)

poem made from words in today’s puzzle
a/ rise hold feel hold break d/ go let go be a/ live live

52D ZERO. Funny, I was thinking about this the other day, when some bozo delivered a speech from behind "George Washington's desk." Our first President lived in private residences in NYC, and conducted business in Federal Hall, before the capitol was moved to Philadelphia. (He took his roll-top desk with him, and later to Mount Vernon.) The city that would later bear his name was still mostly swampland at the time.
There should be a rule against having two crossers each being some obscure singer unless its Sinatra and Martin!
Cute Tuesday! Solved in 9:48.

Interesting fact on the AVOCADO-- The botanical design of the avocado fruit suggests that it evolved to be consumed, digested, then pooped out, fertilized by the nice, rich, um, poop of some bird or animal--sort of what birds do with cherries. But what creature is capable of eating an avocado whole? Well, giant sloths and wooly mammoths, but they went extinct millennia ago. In other words, the avocado hung on, precariously yet just long enough, between the extinction of the megafauna, and its embrace by toast-loving foodies, to survive as a species. Any trained botanist is welcome to elaborate or correct, but I did find this: https://discoverwildscience.com/the-avocados-that-shouldnt-exist-how-humans-saved-a-fruit-designed-for-extinct-megafauna-4-285554/

I had OLEO at first for 32A. It was white in the old days when it first came out, before you mixed in the packets of yellow dye that it came with. Eek!

I did think it odd that Weird Al would write a song about it.

SPOILER ALERT
The Wordplay page includes the column for tomorrow's puzzle, titled "Regards Highly". There's an important spoiler for the whole puzzle right off the top. AMHIK!
Don't look at this column under the illusion that it's about today (Tuesday).
Sigh.

Oh, and one more puzzle find. A Sunday from November 11, 2011 by Joe DiPietro with the title: "Women of the Ear." Some theme answers: ARETHAFLOWERS BARBARASEVILLE MAURATHESAME CORATHEMATTER HEDDALETTUCE DEENASTUDENTS EVADESTRUCTION And there were a couple others. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=11/11/2001&h=16d ...
I loved this theme and this puzzle so much! Adorable theme answers, clean fill and I learned a few things. I especially loved the clever clueing for OREO and NAMES.
https://www.facebook.com/share/1BwWdXFgbX/?mibextid=wwXIfr
This puzzle was made available at 8p pacific time. Why not 7P?

Just marking myself as present.  Nice one today.  Thanks.  

Well, since I have the floor, the games are great but our best time is spent with James Cordon's late night recaps.  Great fun.

Anyone else suddenly seeing thick black lines around the current word to be solved? Using iPhone and the app appears to have updated yesterday. I find it visually distracting. I want to turn the “feature” off. Tried emailing the games address and got a bot. Alas.

@Daniel

Well, REF, but hardly a stumper especially lately.

@Daniel

And OUT, but again not much of a stumper
@Daniel and PAR
But other than those 3, absolutely none.

@Daniel
I see lots of possible sports clues in here:
Players' NAMES appear on uniforms.
An ACE in tennis is unreturnable.
Gung-ho athletes GET UP for the game.
After a tackle, a football player might say, "I'M DOWN."
No player wants to ride the PINE (sit the bench).
Soccer scores often end in ZERO.
At the Indy 500, "Start your engines" means "Turn ON AUTO."
Overconfident players are HOTDOGS.
What would sports be without their FANDOM?
We check our phones for game RESULTS.
The final buzzer means END IT!
After you TEE OFF, you hope to make PAR.
Many elite athletes can say they RAN a marathon.
Race car drivers sometimes do DONUTS after a win.
Golfers often complain about bad LIES.
TCU's college football team is called the HORNED Frogs.
High school and college bands play their Fight SONG.
The last out on the softball diamond means GAME OVER.
And then it's time to pull out the ICE BAG!

@Matt
But before getting the PAR, that golfer has to TEEOFF… (and hope for a good LIE!)
Funny, I thought golf was just another name for socializing while on a walk.

@Puzzled
Is dog sledding not a sport? (“MUSH!”)

@Darcey O’D
True, but it's clued that way in the puzzle. I tried to find  ones that weren't clued as having to do with sports. (I failed on TEE OFF, PAR, and GAME OVER.)

@Jessie

And TEE OFF. Given how I know nothing about golf (and care not to learn anything about it), those two clues were stumpers for most of my solve.
@Jessie golf is not a sport (and he ducks for cover)

@Puzzled I for one don't mind the sports terms that much; where I really suffer is with the players' names, with clues such as "1997 University of Alabama quarterback" (OK, I made that one up, but you get the idea :-)

@Ιασων

I'm with you, let's duck together (which is more of a sport than golf).

@SusanZ

Try updating your app.  Several people have reported success by doing this.

@Steve L

Had exactly the same reaction. Here's Google's Gemini agent's response to the query "Which animals are considered fuzzy?":

The term "fuzzy" generally describes animals with soft, dense, and fine fur or downy feathers that are pleasant to touch.

Mammals
Chinchillas: Thickest fur of all land mammals.
Alpacas: Soft, dense fleece with no lanolin.
Red Pandas: Plush fur including on paw soles.
Sea Otters: Densest fur in the animal kingdom.
Koalas: Thick, woolly fur for rain protection.
Rabbit Kits: Born hairless but quickly grow soft down.
Bumblebee Bats: Tiny bodies covered in fine fur.

Birds (Chicks and Nestlings)
Penguin Chicks: Covered in thick, insulating down feathers.
Ducklings: Soft, yellow down before growing waterproof feathers.
Owl Chicks: Fluffy white down masking adult hunting frames.

Insects
Bumblebees: Covered in pile, which collects pollen grains.
Woolly Bear Caterpillars: Dense, bristly setae resembling miniature carpets.
Domestic Silk Moths: Plump bodies covered in white hair-like scales.

@Steve L

You clearly haven’t been petting the right dogs.

@Xword Junkie
Both BUNNIES and FIREFOXes are on that list.
The rhyme implies that Fuzzy Wuzzy, the BEAR, would be fuzzy ih he had hair, as do other bears.

DOGS, though: not fuzzy.

Liz B,
Thanks for the Part One report. I hope there is a better turnout for Part Two. And please tell David Connell we miss him here!

@SP

'It strikes me that IMDOWN could be clued as “last vertical entry for a very large Ancient Roman crossword?”'

Fuzzy *chested*, eh?

(Enough with the cherry-picking.)

@Eric Hougland

I thought you guys got the puzzles early. Thus writing your reviews early versus real time.

@Eric Hougland

Hey Eric. Just read Kyle’s notes of my July 5th LA Times puzzle. Pass along that “extravehicular activity” is NASA-speak for “space walk”. In my original version that was explained in a revealer but Patti asked that I just move it to the title to make room for an extra themer.

@Weak

Belgium was clearly the better team, but I am still dumbfounded by the U.S. attempt at D. Every four years I have to be reminded that I really don’t like to watch repetitive flop and shin-grab. Probably the next move that will show up in the NBA.

Hi, @Eric!
Haven't seen you here much of late--nice to see you back!

Hi, @Eric!
Haven't seen you here much of late--nice to see you back!

@Weak

Diary of a Crossword Fiend gets a few of the puzzles we regularly review ahead of publication — but not the NYT. 

That's too bad, because it's much easier to work around blogging a puzzle when you can take write it up in advance. 

Much as I've been enjoying the World Cup, I'm glad to see it winding down. So many matches that take up so much of my day — I've got other things to do!

@Andrzej
Based on fishing term, as in a live one on a lure ready to be reeled in. ask me how I know.

@Andrzej 🤣

@Barry Ancona Fun fact: Clement C. Moore was one of the New York Philharmonic’s subscribers in their very first season in 1842. https://archives.nyphil.org/asset-management/2TTUGP9H45HA?&WS=2TTURYFBVA95&Flat=FP

@Joy Harkin --- I assume you mean 18 across, where ENYA crosses LAYNE. I think that qualifies as a Natick and no doubt you're not the only one that needed some help there.

That said, I recommend filing away ENYA as it pops up a lot, very often clued with some combination of "one-named",  "Irish", and/or "new-age" singer.
@jas yes that's what I meant of course. Thx

@jas

ENYA has appeared 161 times, all during the Modern Era, and all clued to the singer, via "Orinoco Flow" 13 times, via Irish or Irleand 29 times, via "one-named" or "mononymous" 34 times, and via "new age" 22 times.

On top of that, she's not particularly obscure in real life, although if you don't listen to popular music, surely you'd have less chance of knowing her.

Her most famous song, "Orinoco Flow," (containing the phrase "Sail away, sail away, sail away") came out in 1988, making her career nearly 40 years long.  It has been a staple on adult contemporary radio for decades.

I believe that you and Joy are both sincere that you don't recognize the name, but I'd say that ENYA is one of the least likely Naticks I can think of.  It's almost like saying that OREO or Yoko ONO is a Natick, which of course means that few solvers would be familiar with her.

@Steve L --- I completely see your point. For me it was a gimmee, but for newer or infrequent solvers it's two very unique proper names crossing each other that either you know or you don't; not really deducible and not names likely to turn up in ones mental Rolodex of friend and foe.

@jas, yeah, ENYA was easy for me (I still have most of her albums on CD!), but I had never heard about anyone named LAYNE, so even when I had LAY__, the only name I could think of with those letters was Layla, which of course didn't work.

@Jeff

@Jeff Z

🤣

@Mean Old Lady

@Nancy

@Nancy

@Vaer  -- It IS AL -- with a lower-case L that looks like an upper-case I.  I always hate that -- don't you?  Here's the L: l. And here's the I: I.

I also hate it when, say, darn (RN) looks like dam (M).  While it doesn't so much on this site's typeface , they always look identical in the clues on the NYT puzzle page.

@Nancy
The lower case RN sometimes gets me, too.

@Bill (cont.)
"45t" refers to page 45, top of page: singers take turns at leading, and will "call" the song selection: "New Britain, 45, top." This video give a good example--singing done in the round, with the leader standing in the center. S/he directs with hand gestures, but the other singers will also use their hands to keep time. Bass parts are usually sung by men, and altos by women, but the tenor parts (which carry the tune), and treble parts, are sung by both men and women, in whatever octave is appropriate. Sing as loud as possible. In a location with good acoustics, and good singers, the "surround sound" for the leader can be quite a moving experience!

"People may say I can't sing, but no one will say I didn't sing."

*This reminds me of Alice's conversation with the White Knight.

@Bill

Thank you; I love backstories like this.

I used to love the song, until 9/11. I worked for a nonprofit in NYC at the time and, in fact, had a clear view of the second tower going down from our balcony. In the days that followed I was our organization's representative at most of the memorials around the city for the first responders, going from one to the other with handfuls of tissues. Amazing Grace was played at each one, on bagpipes. Since then, I cannot listen to it, especially played on bagpipes. I can't listen to bagpipes at all. 

There was a bagpipe concert in our community last weekend. I bravely suggested we attend, thinking it was time I got over myself. Fate stepped in and cancelled the concert, due to weather.

@Bill
"properly"?
An existing melody with new lyrics added always gets a new title from the lyrics.
Danny Boy
The Star-Spangled Banner
Twibkle, Twinkle Little Star
for three examples.

@CP
Yes, "properly." "Amazing Grace" (the poem) was written before "New Britain" (the tune). It was not published with music, and it's uncertain what tune, if any, it was intended to be sung to. It is described merely as "C.M.", or "Common Meter,"or "8;6;8;6"--in other words, it could be sung to any tune with that metrical formula--along with "The Yellow Rose of Texas," "I'd Like to Buy the World a Coke," and almost all the poetry of Emily Dickinson.

Although "New Britain" has been connected to "Amazing Grace" from the start, that is the *only* name it is given in *Southern Harmony* and *The Sacred Harp*, and in most modern hymnals, it is still given as the "tune name," usually over in the top left corner.

I have sung "Londonderry Air" (the tune name for "Danny Boy") to sacred lyrics.

***

I should add that although all of the songs in SH and TSH have religious lyrics, they were not intended for worship service--instead, they were written for performance at, social--yet morally uplifting--gatherings. And not really "performance" (vs. "audience")--participation was the rule, unless you're part of the crew helping ready the potluck "dinner on the grounds."
